title: Events, Observability, Capacity, Inventory, and Cost Control · Language Lens: Event Delivery Semantics
summary: Operators have high-cardinality object-key metrics, duplicate unordered events, incomplete request traces, no inventory reconciliation, and no alert tied to a user or cost outcome. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the event delivery semantics increment in storage telemetry, inventory, capacity, and cost model. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal deb…
objectives: Consume S3 notifications through supported destinations with duplicate, ordering, filtering, recursion, schema, delay, and lost-consumer recovery limits.
Run controlled region, credential, deletion, corruption, dependency, and restore drills with abort, stakeholder communication, integrity checks, and lessons.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Event Delivery Semantics · Theory Event Delivery Semantics
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 4352 during a cost-anomaly investigation. Build deterministic Go evidence to handle duplicate unordered events with bounded telemetry cardinality and cost; record cleanup, rollback, restoration, and residual-risk ownership; then introduce a duplicate event or ambiguous response and reconcile without duplicate effect. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Consume S3 notifications through supported destinations with duplicate, ordering, filtering, recursion, schema, delay, and lost-consumer recovery limits.
